event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a runner's silhouette against an atmospheric city street and an upbeat score, as a male voice states Nike's first-year sales were $8,000 with a $240 profit, contrasting with a female voice's declaration of the company's current $30 billion in sales. A man in a suit speaks intensely before a dramatic red and black visual emphasizes a glowing Nike logo on a sneaker, followed by a sports announcer's exclamation of "Michael Jordan in the NBA!" as a young man in a 'BULLS' jersey stands confidently on a basketball court, leading into the title 'Just Do It: The Inside Story of Nike' sponsored by Shopify. An eerie music and scratching sound introduce the chapter 'FINDING PURPOSE' over sneaker sketches, transitioning to a silhouette of a man in distress against a yellow background with 'NIGHTMARE,' 'EXCEPT,' and 'Reality,' as a narrator explains Phil Knight's nightmare about a purposeless life. The narrator details Phil's time at the University of Oregon, studying journalism and developing an interest in running, introducing track coach Bill Bowerman, who trained Olympic athletes. A montage of historical runners, photos, and a classic Nike sneaker reinforces the brand's roots, with the narrator explaining Phil trained under Bowerman, forming a friendship that became "the foundation of the biggest sports company in the world." The segment concludes with a man in a suit by a window gazing at a vintage photo of runners. The minute opens with the narrator describing Phil, a man in a light suit by a window, as confused about his life, while black-and-white historical runners and text '24 Years old' and 'He had to admit it' represent his dead athletic dreams. A sepia-toned portrait of Phil in a collared shirt with text 'He just hadn’t got to the level needed' and 'HE FELT LOST' emphasizes his struggle, before a dramatic shift to a yellow-toned background with marching soldiers and helicopters, and text 'FRUSTRATED ANGRY,' as the narrator recounts Phil's frustrating army enlistment. A turning point is shown with Phil, wearing a graduation cap with the Stanford University logo and the phrase 'Found his calling,' as the narrator explains his enrollment in business school. A business montage with a lightbulb icon and 'NEW BUSINESS' text leads to a hand writing the question, "Can Japanese Sports Shoes Do to German Sports Shoes What Japanese Cameras Did?" Visuals of cameras on tripods and a map of Germany with Nikon and Canon flags illustrate the camera market disruption, leading to a map of the U.S. with Nike flags and a purple Nike sneaker, as the narrator questions if the same could happen with running shoes. The segment ends with a split screen of PUMA and adidas logos over American and German flags, and a silhouette of a man in triumph against a stock market backdrop, symbolizing Phil's eventual market success.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dark, atmospheric shot of a runner’s silhouette on a city street, accompanied by an upbeat, slightly dramatic score. A male voice recounts Nike's humble beginnings, stating, "First year sales were 1964, we sold $8,000. We made a $240 profit." This transitions to a close-up of a red Nike sneaker with a glowing swoosh, as a female voice highlights the brand's immense growth: "Today, his company Nike has grown in $30 billion in sales last year." The scene shifts to a man in a suit speaking with intensity against blurred Nike branding, followed by a dramatic red and black visual effect emphasizing a glowing Nike logo on a sneaker. An intense, driving musical beat builds with a whoosh sound as a sports announcer exclaims, "Michael Jordan in the NBA!", aligning with the visual of a young man in a 'BULLS' jersey standing confidently on a basketball court. The title 'Just Do It: The Inside Story of Nike's Empire' appears, sponsored by Shopify, with a calm male voice thanking them for sponsoring the video. Atmospheric, slightly eerie music and a scratching sound introduce the chapter title 'FINDING PURPOSE' over sketches of sneakers. A silhouette of a man in distress appears against a yellow background with the word 'NIGHTMARE,' followed by 'EXCEPT' and 'Reality.' A distinct male narrator begins a storytelling segment, explaining, "Phil Knight suddenly woke up. He just been having a nightmare where his life had no purpose... except it wasn't just a dream. This was his reality." The narrator continues, detailing how Phil, unsure what to do, went to the University of Oregon, where he studied journalism and developed his interest in running. The University of Oregon stadium is shown with a runner on a track, introducing Bill Bowerman, labeled as a 'Middle-Distance Runner' in text, and described by the narrator as "the track coach Bill Bowerman, a famous coach in America who trained Olympic athletes." A montage of runners, historical photos, and a close-up of a classic Nike sneaker reinforces the brand’s athletic roots, as the narrator explains Phil trained under Bowerman and they struck up a great friendship, which would later become "the foundation of the biggest sports company in the world." The word 'JOURNALISM' appears over a stadium, linking to Phil's studies. The segment ends with a man in a suit standing by a window, gazing at a vintage photo of runners, symbolizing reflection on Nike’s history.

The minute opens with a male narrator explaining that Phil, seen as a man in a light-colored suit by a window, was confused about his life. A montage of black-and-white historical runners, overlaid with text '24 Years old' and 'He had to admit it,' visually represents Phil's past athletic dreams, which the narrator confirms were dead as he hadn't reached the level needed. A sepia-toned portrait of Phil in a collared shirt, accompanied by text 'He just hadn’t got to the level needed' and 'HE FELT LOST,' reinforces his emotional struggle. The scene dramatically shifts to a yellow-toned background with marching soldiers and helicopters, with text 'FRUSTRATED ANGRY,' as the narrator recounts Phil enlisting in the army and feeling frustrated. A turning point is marked by Phil, now wearing a graduation cap with the Stanford University logo and the phrase 'Found his calling,' as the narrator explains he enrolled in business school there. A business-themed montage with a lightbulb icon and 'NEW BUSINESS' text leads to a hand writing a pivotal question on paper, which the narrator reads aloud: "Can Japanese Sports Shoes Do to German Sports Shoes What Japanese Cameras Did?" This is followed by visuals of cameras on tripods and a map of Germany with Nikon and Canon flags, illustrating how Japanese camera companies had replaced German ones, as the narrator explains. The narrative then transitions to a map of the U.S. with Nike flags and a purple Nike sneaker with 'running shoes' text, as the narrator wonders if the same disruption could happen with running shoes. The segment concludes with a split screen showing PUMA and adidas logos over American and German flags, representing the leading German running shoe brands, and a silhouette of a man raising his arm in triumph against a stock market backdrop, symbolizing the eventual market success of Phil's vision. Throughout, a consistent male narrator guides the story, accompanied by background music.

event_blocks.summary: A narrator explains Phil's belief that producing shoes in Japan would be cheaper and that the Japanese were experimenting with new designs, a concept illustrated by a montage featuring a Japanese flag and a sneaker, overlaid with text about production costs. In a classroom setting, a chalkboard displays PUMA, adidas, Canon, and Nikon logos connected by red pins and dashed lines, symbolizing a strategic network, as the narrator details Phil's significant investment in a business class assignment focused on importing Japanese shoes to the American market, visualized by maps of Japan and the United States. Phil, a man in a collared shirt, is shown covering his face in frustration as a classmate gestures dramatically, reflecting the skepticism Phil faced regarding his idea. A glowing lightbulb with a sneaker inside, surrounded by mathematical equations, signifies innovation, while Phil reappears with a determined expression, the word 'himself' appearing on screen, prompting the narrator to question the challenge of pursuing the idea alone due to Phil's lack of contacts and import knowledge. A red background with 'JAPAN' and 'Japanese shoes $$$' emphasizes cost and market potential. The video cuts to a black screen with the text 'Could he really JUST DO IT?' and the narrator's direct question, accompanied by swelling and fading background music. After a brief silence, the sound of a pen writing, a sharp click, and a whoosh precede a title card: 'CHAPTER 2: A TRIP TO THE FUTURE.' Graduates toss caps in front of the University of Oregon Stadium, with '1962' displayed, as the narrator explains that after graduating business school in 1962, Phil returned home, living with his parents and feeling his life was passing him by, underscored by a dramatic, melancholic musical score.

raw_segments: The video opens with a dynamic montage of abstract visuals, including a Japanese flag and a close-up of a sneaker, overlaid with text stating 'He also knew that it would be cheaper to produce shoes in Japan as well.' A clear male narrator explains that Phil had heard the Japanese were experimenting with new shoe designs and that production would be cheaper there. The scene transitions to a classroom setting with a chalkboard displaying logos of PUMA, adidas, Canon, and Nikon, connected by red pins and dashed lines, symbolizing a strategic network, as the narrator details Phil's extreme investment in this business class assignment, genuinely believing in the opportunity to import Japanese shoes into the American market. Maps of Japan ('Japanese shoes') and the United States ('American market') appear, illustrating this global business strategy. A man in a collared shirt, identified by the narrator as Phil, is shown covering his face in frustration or realization, while another man gestures dramatically in the background, as the narrator recounts how Phil's idea was met with skepticism by his classmates and friends. A glowing lightbulb with a sneaker inside, surrounded by mathematical equations, emphasizes innovation. Phil reappears with a determined expression, as the text 'himself' appears, and the narrator questions the difficulty of pursuing this idea alone, given Phil's lack of contacts or knowledge about importing and selling. A bold red background with 'JAPAN' and 'Japanese shoes $$$' highlights cost and market potential. The video then cuts to a black screen with the text 'Could he really JUST DO IT?', accompanied by the narrator's direct question, "So, could he really just do it?", as the background music swells and then fades. After a brief silence, the distinct sound of a pen writing is heard, followed by a sharp click and a whoosh, leading to a title card: 'CHAPTER 2: A TRIP TO THE FUTURE.' The final scenes show graduates tossing caps in front of the University of Oregon Stadium, with the year '1962' displayed, as the narrator explains that after graduating from business school in 1962, Phil found himself back home, living with his parents, feeling that familiar sense of his life just passing him by, all set to a dramatic, melancholic musical score.

event_blocks.summary: Initially contemplative on a yoga mat amidst abstract light streaks, Phil, a Man in Suit, grappled with the need for meaningful action, rejecting the idea of waiting for a 'magic opportunity.' He held a glowing red orb, symbolizing his vision, as a Photographer with Camera documented his decision to create his own path, embarking on a world trip. This journey, driven by a 'crazy idea' to introduce Japanese shoes to America, included visits to the Colosseum and the Temple of Nike, linking to the goddess of victory. A pivotal shift occurred on a Tropical Beach Scene, marked by a toast and upbeat music, signifying a turning point. Despite initial shyness and poor performance in a door-to-door sales job in Honolulu, Phil's friend stayed behind as Phil decided it was 'time to leave,' facing an internal struggle. Later, against a red and black grid, Phil knew his next destination was Japan, drawn by the Onitsuka Tiger sneakers he discovered in Kobe. He memorized 'HOW TO DO BUSINESS WITH THE JAPANESE: A STRATEGY FOR SUCCESS' from a book before cold-calling the owners, arranging a meeting. Despite having 'zero expertise,' Phil presented himself as an American shoe distributor to market their high-quality, cheap shoes in America, aligning with Onitsuka's desire for a U.S. market presence, culminating in a symbolic image of Asian Business Professionals before a U.S. dollar bill.

Tropical Beach Scene.description: A vibrant Hawaiian beach with palm trees, boats, and turquoise water, symbolizing a new beginning and a change in environment, where Phil and his friend surfed, relaxed, and even took jobs selling encyclopedias door-to-door to extend their stay in this 'Hawaiian paradise.' This scene is accompanied by upbeat, energetic, slightly tropical music with wave sound effects.

Silhouette of Man in Triumph.description: A dark silhouette of a man raising his arm in victory, briefly appearing against a colorful stock market backdrop, symbolizing success and market triumph, though it is not the central focus in this segment.

He is shown in various emotional states—nervous, excited, and determined—as he decides to pursue his dream in Japan, cold-calls the shoe brand owners, and prepares for a meeting despite having "zero expertise" in the business, introducing himself as an American shoe distributor.

The audio reveals Phil's interest in these "high-quality" yet "relatively cheap" shoes, leading him to cold-call the owners to arrange a meeting and offer to market their shoes in America.

Book Cover.description: A red book titled 'HOW TO DO BUSINESS WITH THE JAPANESE: A STRATEGY FOR SUCCESS,' shown in a bookstore setting. The audio explicitly states Phil bought this book and "memorized as much information as he could" to prepare for his business venture in Japan.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a man, identified by the narrator as Phil, in a suit sitting contemplatively on a yoga mat, head in hands, against a vibrant abstract background of purple and red light streaks, as the narrator explains Phil needed to do something meaningful. The scene transitions to a black and white close-up of Phil with a hand covering his face, as the word 'MAGIC' appears, aligning with the narrator's description of Phil realizing he couldn't just wait for a 'magic opportunity.' Phil is then seen holding a glowing red orb, symbolizing his powerful vision, while a photographer captures the moment, as the narrator details Phil's decision to create an opportunity for himself and embark on a world trip with a friend. The orb reappears in a red circle with text, reinforcing Phil's 'crazy idea' of bringing Japanese shoes to the American market, which he hoped to make 'into a reality' by visiting Japan. Their journey is visually represented by iconic landmarks like the Colosseum in Rome and the Temple of Nike in Greece, which the narrator explicitly mentions as places they visited, linking to the goddess of victory. The narrative dramatically shifts to a tropical Hawaiian beach, with the text 'That’s where things CHANGED!' as two people toast, accompanied by upbeat, energetic, slightly tropical music with wave sound effects, signifying a turning point. A surfer rides a wave, and a man in a Hawaiian shirt stands on a dock, embracing the new environment. The scene returns to Phil in his suit, now labeled 'Phil was pretty shy,' against a red grid, with a silhouette of him walking away with a suitcase, as the narrator reveals Phil's shyness and poor performance at a sales job selling encyclopedias door-to-door in Honolulu, which he and his friend took to extend their stay in 'Hawaiian paradise.' The word 'LEAVE' appears, followed by 'REVEAL,' as the narrator states Phil decided it was 'time to leave,' while his friend stayed behind. The final frame shows Phil looking conflicted with a hand on his head, as 'REVEAL' remains on screen, capturing his internal struggle and impending decision.

The segment opens with a dramatic, stylized shot of the Man in Suit, identified as 'Phil,' against a red and black grid, as a male narrator states that Phil "already knew exactly where he wanted to go next." Driving background music begins as the scene transitions to a purple-tinted park and then a quick montage of Japanese urban life, including a highway underpass and a red double-decker bus, while the narrator explains it was "time to go to Japan." The focus shifts to a purple-lit retail display of Onitsuka Tiger sneakers, with the brand logo prominent, as the narrator details how Phil discovered a particular running shoe in Kobe in 1962, noting the brand was called 'Tiger' and manufactured by 'Onitsuka.' Text overlays emphasize 'two main criteria: quality' and 'me,' aligning with the narrator's explanation that the shoes were "clearly high-quality" yet "relatively cheap." Phil is then shown on the phone, looking nervous and excited, with the Onitsuka Tiger logo superimposed, as the narrator reveals he "cold-called the owners" to arrange a meeting. He appears in front of Japanese magazines and books, including a red cover titled 'HOW TO DO BUSINESS WITH THE JAPANESE: A STRATEGY FOR SUCCESS,' which the narrator confirms Phil bought and "memorized as much information as he could" before his meeting. The video concludes with a group of confident Asian business professionals in front of a large U.S. dollar bill, symbolizing success, as the Onitsuka Tiger logo and Phil’s image fade in and out, while the narrator explains Phil, despite "zero expertise," introduced himself as an American shoe distributor to market their shoes in America, and that Onitsuka "had been looking for a way."

event_blocks.summary: Phil, the Man in Suit, stood before blurred Asian Business Professionals, holding books, as a glowing purple 'SAME IDEA' circle appeared, confirming his vision to sell Japanese shoes in America, a concept met with Japanese excitement. Visually confirmed as a recent university graduate in his twenties with no business experience, indicated by Phil in a graduation cap with fireworks, the narrative highlighted his data-driven planning through sticky notes with hand-drawn charts and sneaker sketches, following a red strike-through over 'Onitsuka' and the emergence of 'EXPERIENCE.' Phil, shown in black and white pointing at a graph, was revealed to have memorized extensive facts and statistics from university assignments, impressing Japanese managers with his apparent knowledge. A Japanese manager's question, 'What’s the name of the company you’re from?' prompted a thoughtful gesture from Phil, whose heartbeat quickened as the text revealed he didn't have a company, only a 'crazy idea' to bring 'JAPANESE SHOES TO AMERICA.' His mind flashed to a house with the text 'BACK AT HOME,' and the narrative concluded in a purple-lit room with blue streamers, where Phil, arms outstretched, was inspired by childhood blue ribbons, marking the birth of 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS' after a brief pause.

Onitsuka Tiger.description: A brand of Japanese athletic footwear, prominently displayed in a retail setting with purple lighting, featuring sneakers in pink, black, and white, and the logo appearing multiple times to emphasize its significance in Phil’s business strategy. In this segment, the brand name 'Onitsuka' is visually struck through, symbolizing a transition away from it toward a new identity.

The narrator explains these streamers are reminiscent of the blue ribbons from Phil's childhood that he used to have hung up on his wall, connecting his past to the birth of 'Blue Ribbon Sports'.

The narrator confirms he was a recent graduate in his twenties with no actual business experience but had memorized extensive facts about the American shoe market.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Phil, the Man in Suit, standing confidently before blurred business professionals, holding books, as a glowing purple 'SAME IDEA' circle appears, signifying his shared vision to sell Japanese shoes in America, a concept the Japanese were excited about. The scene transitions to Phil in a graduation cap with fireworks, visually confirming 'HE QUITE LITERALLY WAS' a recent university graduate in his twenties with no actual business experience, as the narrator explains. Despite this, a red strike-through over 'Onitsuka' and the emergence of 'EXPERIENCE' is followed by sticky notes with hand-drawn charts and sketches of sneakers, illustrating Phil's data-driven planning. He is then shown in black and white, pointing at a graph, with the text 'HE COMPLETELY KNEW WHAT HE WAS TALKING ABOUT,' as the narrator details how Phil had memorized extensive facts and statistics from his university assignments, impressing the Japanese managers. The narrative shifts to a modern office where a male voice, a Japanese manager, asks, 'What’s the name of the company you’re from?' Phil, now in grayscale, responds with a thoughtful gesture, his heartbeat suddenly quickening, as the text reveals 'HE DIDN’T ACTUALLY HAVE A COMPANY YET,' only a 'crazy idea' to bring 'JAPANESE SHOES TO AMERICA.' His mind races, wanting to be back home, a house appearing with the text 'BACK AT HOME.' The final scene shows Phil in a purple-lit room with blue streamers, arms outstretched, as the narrator explains his mind flashed to the blue ribbons from his childhood, marking the birth of his new venture, 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS,' after a second's pause.

event_blocks.summary: Blue Ribbon Sports of Portland, Oregon, was founded by Phil, who, after pitching the American market and Onitsuka's Tiger shoes, agreed to become the Japanese company's US distribution partner, a deal visually confirmed by celebrating professionals and a map of the US marked with Nike flags, signifying the imminent reality of his 'crazy idea.' A month passed with no shoe samples arriving, causing Phil distress as he had written to Onitsuka and paid $50, a sum his father revealed was likely lost, raising the question of whether Phil had been scammed. However, 12 sample pairs of shoes eventually arrived, meeting Phil's hopes, and he sought out his former University of Oregon running coach, Bill Bowerman, a respected figure obsessed with improving athletes' shoes. Bowerman, known for cutting open student shoes to make adjustments like adding cushion or using lighter materials, sometimes caused pain but often improved shoes for better or more comfortable running, a process symbolized by a runner's improved performance. Bowerman's expertise led him to become Phil's business partner, forming Blue Ribbon Sports, with each investing $500 to order 300 pairs of shoes from Japan at $3.33 per pair, a shipment that arrived in April 1964. Phil secured exclusive distributor status for the Western United States, but faced the significant challenge of finding a place to sell the 300 Tiger shoes.

Onitsuka Tiger.description: A brand of Japanese athletic footwear, prominently displayed with a white sneaker featuring blue and red stripes, and the brand's logo appearing in a stylized white speech bubble. The narrator refers to these as the 'new Japanese running shoes' and 'Tiger shoes' that Bowerman liked and Phil sought to distribute.

This environment appears toward the end of the segment, marking Phil’s return home and the birth of his new venture, 'Blue Ribbon Sports.'

Bowerman is described as the 'perfect partner' due to his experience training athletes and obsession with track shoe construction. Phil is the one facing the problem of selling the shoes.

These appear in the middle of the segment, visually representing Phil’s analytical approach to launching his business.

Clock Face with '1 MONTH'.description: A close-up of a clock face with the text '1 MONTH' overlaid, indicating the passage of time and marking a significant milestone in the narrative, which the narrator emphasizes by counting 'One month, two months, three months, four months,' highlighting the growing concern as 'no shoe samples arrived'.

University of Oregon Stadium.description: A large stadium with a prominent 'O' logo, shown under purple lighting, serving as the backdrop for Bill Bowerman’s shoe modifications, reinforcing its role as the birthplace of Nike’s innovation, where Bowerman would take student shoes to cut them open.

Bill Bowerman.description: A historical figure shown in a sepia-toned portrait with a bow tie, labeled as a 'Middle-Distance Runner,' later seen actively cutting open a shoe at the University of Oregon. The narrator describes him as Phil's old running coach, 'highly respected in the world of running,' and 'obsessed with improving athletes' shoes,' who would 'take student shoes and cut them open and make adjustments' like 'adding more cushion or using more lightweight materials,' ultimately making them 'better or more comfortable for running.'

The narrator directly vocalizes this, stating, 'There was just one problem... What Phil didn't have was anywhere to sell the shoes.'

raw_segments: The video opens with a grayscale image of Phil, the Man in Suit, gesturing expansively against a purple background, as a narrator introduces "Blue Ribbon Sports of Portland, Oregon," explaining that Phil "just kind of blurted the name out of nowhere" and "never expected to get this far." A split-screen shows Phil and a younger man in professional settings, suggesting strategic planning, while the narrator details Phil's increasing confidence as he pitched the American market and the unique "Onitsuka's Tiger shoes." A close-up of a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker with blue and red stripes appears, followed by its logo, as the narrator describes the Japanese company's eagerness to make Phil their distribution partner in the US, to which he "obviously agreed." A group of diverse professionals, including a smiling Asian man, celebrate, visually confirming the agreement, as a map of the United States pinned with a red thumbtack and images of Onitsuka Tiger sneakers appear, with text declaring Phil's "crazy idea was about to become a reality." The scene fades to black, transitioning to a title card: 'CHAPTER 3: ONE BECOMES TWO.' A clock ticking sound accompanies the visual of a clock face with '1 MONTH' and a calendar showing January 1, as the narrator counts "One month, two months, three months, four months," emphasizing that "no shoe samples arrived." Phil reappears with a distressed, fragmented visual effect, having returned to America "so excited and optimistic," only to be confused by the delay. A close-up shows a hand writing 'WHAT WAS GOING ON WITH THE SAMPLE,' directly following the narrator's mention that Phil "wrote to them," with a torn envelope displaying '50 DOLLARS' hinting at the financial or logistical crux of the situation.

The segment opens with a close-up of a torn envelope containing a handwritten note that reads 'Shoes coming, In just a little more days,' which the narrator reads aloud, explaining Phil was waiting for samples he'd paid $50 for. The scene transitions to a sepia-toned portrait of a man with a mustache and cap, identified as Phil's dad, who chuckles and states, "Son, that 50 bucks is long gone," as the text 'SON, THAT FIFTY BUCKS IS LONG GONE' overlays the image, suggesting a financial loss. A bold question, 'HAD PHIL REALLY BEEN SCAMMED?' appears, posed by the narrator, setting a tone of doubt. The narrative shifts to a modern-day delivery woman in a blue cap and white shirt, driving a van filled with shoe boxes, delivering them to a building, as the narrator announces that "12 sample pairs of shoes from Japan arrived on his doorstep." Inside, a man in a suit receives the boxes with satisfaction, confirmed by the narrator stating, "They were just like he'd hoped," while the text 'TWELVE SAMPLE PAIRS OF SHOES' appears. The scene cuts to a historical figure, Bill Bowerman, shown in a sepia portrait, with the narrator explaining Phil went to see his old running coach, who "was highly respected in the world of running" and "obsessed with improving athletes' shoes." Bowerman is then seen at the University of Oregon, cutting open a shoe, with the text 'CUT THEM OPEN MAKE ADJUSTMENTS,' as the narrator details how he would "take student shoes and cut them open and make adjustments." An animated diagram of a shoe highlights modifications like 'Adding more cushion or using more lightweight materials,' which the narrator describes as examples of Bowerman's work. A runner on a track is shown in pain, with the text 'JUST MADE THEIR FEET PAINFUL,' contrasting with the narrator's comment that sometimes experiments "just made their feet painful." This is followed by a shot of a woman running comfortably, with the text 'IMPROVED THE SHOES BETTER OR MORE COMFORTABLE,' as the narrator concludes that "quite often, Bowerman improved the shoes and made them better or more comfortable for running." The segment ends with a man in a white shirt and cap, smiling, as the word 'BETTER' appears, symbolizing successful innovation.

The video opens with a dynamic montage of historical runners in sepia tones, as a narrator explains how Bill Bowerman "really knew his stuff when it came to running shoes," and how Phil had been keen to hear his opinion on his "new Japanese running shoes." A close-up of a blue Asics sneaker with a white logo appears, followed by a handshake between two men in suits, symbolizing the business agreement after Bowerman liked the sample shoes so much he offered to be Phil's business partner. The scene transitions to a man in a suit examining a shoe, with text 'PERFECT PARTNER' and 'TRAINING ATHLETES' appearing over abstract purple and gold paint splashes, as the narrator elaborates that Bowerman was indeed the "perfect partner," having spent his life training athletes and being obsessed with track shoe construction. The narrative shifts to a storefront labeled 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES', indicating the launch of the new company, Blue Ribbon Sports, co-founded by Phil and Bowerman, each investing $500. A conveyor belt of shoes and text overlays revealing '300 PAIRS OF SHOES' at '$3.33 A PAIR' emphasize the initial small-scale operation and affordability, as the narrator confirms this money was used to order 300 pairs from Japan at roughly $3.33 per pair. The story progresses with a map of the U.S. highlighting 'EXCLUSIVE DISTRIBUTOR' status, as Phil wrote to Onitsuka asking for Blue Ribbon Sports to be the exclusive distributor of these Tiger shoes in the Western United States. This is followed by a cargo ship labeled 'IN THE YEAR 1964' carrying a shipment of '300 TIGER SHOES', which the narrator confirms arrived from Japan in April 1964. The final frames show a man in a suit with a concerned expression, overlaid with text 'THERE WAS JUST ONE PROBLEM' and 'IS ANYWHERE TO SELL THE SHOES', as the narrator concludes that while Phil now had a supplier, a partner, and shoes, "What Phil didn't have was anywhere to sell the shoes." The segment ends with a close-up of a shoe with a purple circle, symbolizing the core product and the looming business hurdle.

event_blocks.summary: Initially, Phil Knight's attempts to sell Onitsuka Tiger shoes were met with skepticism, as retailers had 'no need for another track shoe,' prompting him to realize he 'was going to have to sell these shoes himself.' Selling from the trunk of his car at track meets and running clubs, Knight's personal belief in the product resonated with customers, leading to the sale of 'all 300 pairs within just 3 months,' a stark contrast to his past failures selling encyclopedias. By the end of their first year, Blue Ribbon Sports achieved significant financial success, selling 8,000 pairs and earning enough to order '900 more pairs from Japan.' Knight recruited sales representatives, 'mostly ex-runners,' who worked 'solely on commission' earning '$2 for every pair of shoes they sold,' and shared the company's vision. This expansion allowed Blue Ribbon Sports to move 'out of Phil's parents' basement and into its first real office.' Jeff Johnson, hired as the company's first full-time employee, would later conceive the iconic name 'Nike.' With sales rising rapidly, Blue Ribbon Sports opened its own retail store in 1966, designed as a 'sanctuary for runners' to 'hang out and talk,' attracting significant attention. While Phil and Johnson managed the business operations, Bill Bowerman focused on creative aspects, including tinkering with shoe designs, taking shoes from Japan, and 'make notes on how to improve them.' Bowerman also played a pivotal role in popularizing jogging through his book 'JOGGING,' which sold over a million copies, and by coaching runners. His design for the Onitsuka Tiger Cortez became the highest-selling shoe in 1969, contributing to Blue Ribbon's revenue of around '$300,000.'

raw_segments: The segment opens with a purple Nike sneaker prominently displayed, overlaid with the text 'THEY DIDN’T TAKE PHIL SERIOUSLY,' as a male narrator explains how Phil Knight's initial attempts to sell his shoes to local sporting goods stores were met with rejection, stating, "they didn't take Phil seriously" and "had no need for another track shoe." A diagram appears, showing Phil Knight's face connected to rejected stores, leading him to realize he "was going to have to sell these shoes himself." A sepia-toned Phil, now with white hair, holds a shoe as the text 'SELL THESE SHOES' appears, emphasizing his determination. The title 'CHAPTER 4: BLUERIBBON' emerges, surrounded by sneaker sketches, marking a new phase. The narrative shifts to Phil selling shoes from the trunk of his car in front of a Kmart sign, with the narrator detailing how he "began going to track meets and running clubs, and started selling the shoes from the trunk of his car." A montage of runners on a track, accompanied by the text 'TO RUNNERS,' shows Phil enthusiastically presenting the shoes to athletes, coaches, and fans. This grassroots effort, combined with his commitment, led to selling "all 300 pairs within just 3 months," highlighted by a smiling man in sunglasses and bold text. The narrator draws a parallel to Phil's past, noting that while he "failed horribly" selling encyclopedias door-to-door, selling these running shoes "seemed strangely easy." A close-up of a hand holding a purple Nike sneaker appears with text emphasizing 'HIS PERSONAL BELIEF IN THE PRODUCT' and how it 'MADE PEOPLE WANNA BUY THEM,' as the narrator confirms customers could "sense his personal belief in the product," making them want to buy. The segment concludes with a calendar showing 'FIRST YEAR' and a clock face, followed by a 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES' storefront, where money rains down, symbolizing financial success. The narrator reveals that "by the end of their first year, Blue Ribbon Sports had sold 8,000" pairs, marking a triumphant breakthrough. Throughout, a light, evolving instrumental background music underscores the narrative, shifting from skepticism to determination and finally to success, punctuated by subtle sound effects.

The video opens with a dynamic montage of money raining over a cargo ship labeled 'MAERSK,' symbolizing global commerce and financial success, as the narrator explains Phil used his earnings to order "900 more pairs from Japan," aligning with the bold text '900 MORE.' The scene transitions to a man in a suit holding a tablet, with the text 'MOSTLY EX-RUNNERS' appearing, as the narrator details the recruitment of sales reps, mostly former athletes who "shared their vision" and worked "solely on commission." A close-up of hands counting money is shown with the text 'WHO SHARED THE VISION,' emphasizing alignment and purpose, while the narrator specifies they earned "$2 for every pair of shoes they sold," reinforced by the repeated '$2' text. The narrative shifts to a woman in a white shirt, then a map of the U.S. with a dotted line from Philadelphia to California appears, labeled 'ALLOWED PHI,' as the narrator describes their expansion across America and how their success led to a business loan, allowing them to move "Blue Ribbon Sports out of Phil's parents' basement and into its first real office." This is followed by a group of excited people celebrating and a modern office environment with digital icons, labeled 'FULL-TIME JOB,' showing professional growth. A man in a plaid shirt is shown with the text 'JEFF JOHNSON,' identified by the narrator as "the company's first full-time employee" hired because Bowerman "still had a full-time job," and notably, "the man who would later go on to come up with the company's iconic name, Nike." The video then highlights a man with white hair, with text 'THEY WERE STILL CALLED BLUE RIBBON SPORTS,' as the narrator confirms that "for now, they were still called Blue Ribbon Sports." A bar graph showing sales growth from 1964 to 1966 appears, with rising orange bars and a purple line, illustrating financial success, as the narrator states that "with sales rising rapidly, in 1966, they were able to open their own retail store to sell their shoes." The scene transitions to a store interior with the 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS' logo, where people are browsing, and text 'DESIGNED THE STORE TO BE' appears, emphasizing strategic retail design, as the narrator explains they "designed the store to be a sanctuary for runners," a place to "hang out and talk" and browse books, which "helped them attract a lot of attention." A man in a suit is shown with text 'WORKED ON THE BUSINESS SIDE,' highlighting operational focus, as the narrator clarifies that "Phil and the new employee Johnson worked on the business side of the operations," while "Bowerman was handling the creative" aspects. The video ends with a close-up of a hand sketching shoe designs on paper, labeled 'Shoe Design Ideas,' with a red circle and scissors, symbolizing innovation and product development.

event_blocks.summary: A collage of vintage images, including runners and a man holding a shoe, alongside a "BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES" storefront overlaid with '$300,000' and text about success, introduced a narrator explaining the booming business between Blue Ribbon Sports and Onitsuka, symbolized by juxtaposed images of men in suits. This transitioned to a green digital sequence with the Nike logo, a man's silhouette facing a glowing Earth, and cascading money, signaling global ambition, as ominous music played and the narrator revealed Onitsuka's betrayal. The tone shifted to reflection on entrepreneurial struggles, with Phil starting by selling shoes from his car, before an upbeat track introduced Shopify as a solution for online business creation, demonstrated by a woman at a laptop in a home office and Shopify's interface with the tagline 'If you can dream it, you can sell it with Shopify.' The narrator highlighted Shopify's ease of use for creating professional online stores and receiving payments, further illustrated by a man in glasses surrounded by digital effects and floating dollar bills, as he shared his personal goal of having an online business enabling global travel, culminating in a white silhouette of a man with a shopping bag icon and the Shopify logo. The narrative then shifted to 'CHAPTER 5: BETRAYAL,' detailing Phil's request to Onitsuka for exclusive US distribution of Tiger shoes, met with a rejection symbolized by a map of North America flashing 'NO!' and text about skyscrapers, followed by Phil's false claim of East Coast offices. Despite this, Onitsuka was impressed and struck a deal for Blue Ribbon to be the exclusive US partner, marked by a milestone of 20,000 Onitsuka Tiger shoes sold. The segment continued with a purple-tinted map of the US showing shipping boxes for an order of '20,000 more pairs,' and Phil, in a dimly lit office, sending out 'INVEST IN BRS NOW!' flyers to raise money for new offices, which received no response, visually represented by a man covering his face. However, the parents of an early employee, Bob Woodell, invested their 'entire life savings' of $8,000, despite a visual of '$95,000' before a bank facade, as banks refused further loans. Jeff Johnson appeared frustrated, and the segment concluded with a split-screen of two older men and a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker, underscoring financial hardship linked to the product and supplier. The narrative then detailed Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman's love for Onitsuka shoes and the brand's formidable presence in the US thanks to Blue Ribbon Sports, but revealed 'problems' and instability symbolized by a Jenga tower. Phil constantly worried about cash flow for expansion, needing more facilities and staff, as sales, particularly of the 'Cortez' model, outpaced supply, leading Phil to repeatedly request faster shipments from Onitsuka, who prioritized their Japanese customers. The tension escalated with Phil, in a dimly lit office, feeling powerless as Onitsuka was selling leftover stock to the US. The segment shifted to Phil's tense meeting with Onitsuka, where suggestions for design improvements were ignored, infuriating Bowerman. A formal dinner scene preceded Phil's meeting with 'A man named Katami,' who expressed disappointment in Blue Ribbon's sales and lacked interest in renewing the contract, despite Phil feeling his company had earned an extension, especially given Onitsuka's success in America was down to Blue Ribbon Sports and Bowerman's design contributions. Suspicions arose about Onitsuka's plans to partner with other distributors, culminating in a man in a tuxedo looking alarmed with a warning triangle. The narrative progressed to 'CHAPTER 6: DON’T GET ANGRY, GET EVEN,' where Phil, after recovering from shock, decided to 'strike back,' realizing that losing Onitsuka Tiger and the 'Cortez' was an opportunity to create their own shoes. The team, including Phil and Bowerman, decided to work on original designs, resolving the import issues. After considering 'Dimension 6,' Jeff Johnson proposed the name 'Nike,' derived from the Greek goddess of victory. Phil, initially hesitant, agreed due to time constraints, symbolized by a fiery hourglass. The next step was a logo, created by a young woman for '$35,' resulting in the iconic white Nike swoosh. The search for a shoe manufacturer led to discussions at 'Midway Sports & Loan' and a workshop where a new version of the Cortez was being produced, but initial manufacturers faced 'QUALITY CONTROL ISSUES.'

Chapter 5 Title Card.description: A bold title card reading 'CHAPTER 5: BETRAYAL' in black text on a white background, with sneaker sketches in the corners, introducing a dramatic turning point in the narrative.

Map of U.S. with Nike Flags.description: A map of the United States with Nike flags, representing the potential for Japanese sports shoes to disrupt the American market and capture a new audience, now shown with the U.S. highlighted in orange to indicate the scope of the distribution challenge.

World Map with U.S. Highlighted.description: A purple-tinted world map with North America highlighted in yellow, used to visually represent the geographical scope of the distribution dispute, as the narrator recounts Phil's claim about East Coast offices.

Man with White Hair.description: A man with white hair and a mustache, identified as Phil (Knight) by the narrator, is shown in a purple-tinted scene. Text overlays about cash flow risks align with the narrator's explanation that Phil was in a risky situation, constantly worrying about cash flow to support increasing stores and employees.

Onitsuka Tiger.description: A white sneaker with blue and red stripes, specifically identified as the 'Cortez,' Onitsuka's best-selling shoe, which was designed by Bowerman, symbolizing the product at the heart of the business struggle and the reason for the strategic shift.

Skyscraper Background.description: A low-angle shot of modern skyscrapers under an orange sky, symbolizing corporate power, appears as the narrator explains Onitsuka's initial rejection of Phil's company due to its perceived lack of size and establishment.

Couple in Modest Home.description: An older man and woman in a dimly lit, modest home, counting cash in bins, with the man holding a phone, identified by the narrator as the parents of early employee Bob Woodell, who invested $8,000, described as their 'entire life savings,' to support the struggling company.

Jeff Johnson.description: A man in a plaid shirt, shown in a close-up with a frustrated expression, later in a backyard looking downcast, representing the personal toll of business failure.

Promotional Flyers.description: Stacked white flyers with orange and purple text reading 'INVEST IN BRS NOW!' and 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS', symbolizing the failed fundraising effort.

Split-Screen of Older Men.description: Two older men in a purple-tinted split-screen, one with white hair and a mustache, the other with a stern expression, symbolizing leadership in the early days of Blue Ribbon Sports, with an Onitsuka Tiger sneaker and text overlays emphasizing mutual respect and a win-win partnership.

Bank Facade.description: A stylized bank building with columns, displaying '$95,000' in bold yellow text, visually representing the 'entire life savings' mentioned by the narrator as invested in Blue Ribbon Sports.

Patterned Shoe Background.description: A beige background covered in repeating purple shoe silhouettes, used as a visual motif for the brand's identity and the challenges of production and distribution, appearing behind key figures and text overlays.

Map of U.S. and Japan.description: An orange map of the U.S. and Japan, shown side-by-side with a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker flying between them, symbolizing the international supply chain and the conflict between domestic and Japanese market priorities.

Man in Blue Shirt.description: A man in a blue checkered shirt, shown in a purple-tinted setting, touching his nose thoughtfully. The voiceover identifies him as 'Phil,' describing him as 'outraged' and 'hurt,' and unable to 'believe this was happening,' representing a moment of deep contemplation and concern about the business's direction and the betrayal.

Cargo Ship with Shipment.description: A large cargo ship sailing on the ocean, symbolizing the problematic international supply chain and distribution issues, which the new strategy of making their own shoes aims to circumvent by avoiding waiting for shipments.

Man in Tan Jacket.description: A man in a tan jacket, walking through a dark, industrial setting with a purple tint, represents the operational challenges of scaling the business. The text 'HIRE MORE STAFF' aligns with the narrator's statement that Phil had to get more facilities, hire more staff, and order more shoes.

Jenga Tower.description: A wooden Jenga tower with blocks labeled 'A', 'B', 'BB', 'BBB', shown in a dimly lit setting, symbolizes the fragile balance of the business's growth and financial stability. The narrator explains this instability arose from the rapid opening of new stores and the constant worry about cash flow.

Man in Tuxedo.description: A man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, shown in a dimly lit, purple-tinted setting, looking alarmed, symbolizing the realization of betrayal and the breakdown of trust, with a red warning triangle and text 'SOMETHING WASN’T RIGHT' emphasizing the crisis.

Bill Bowerman.description: A historical figure shown in a sepia-toned portrait with a bow tie, labeled as a 'Middle-Distance Runner,' later seen actively cutting open a shoe at the University of Oregon, symbolizing his hands-on approach to innovation, now depicted in a new context as designing Onitsuka Tiger shoes and coaching athletes, with text emphasizing his contributions to improving shoe designs. The narrator confirms his name and describes him as 'infuriated' by Onitsuka ignoring suggestions, having 'worked hard on new designs and improvements.'

Silhouette of Man in Triumph.description: A dark silhouette of a man raising his arm in victory, briefly appearing against a colorful stock market backdrop, symbolizing success and market triumph, though it is not the central focus in this segment.

Text Overlay 'ONE PROBLEM'.description: Bold orange text reading 'THERE WASN’T REALLY MUCH PHIL COULD DO' and 'SUGGESTIONS' and 'THAT PHIL AND BOWERMAN SHARED WITH THEM,' overlaid on dark backgrounds, highlighting the critical challenges and the efforts made to address them.

Man Named Kitagawa.description: A silhouette of a man in a suit against a purple background, labeled 'THE MAN NAMED KITAGAWA,' representing the Onitsuka representative whose strange behavior and lack of interest in renewing the deal signal the beginning of the partnership's collapse.

Handshake Graphic.description: A yellow handshake icon connecting the Onitsuka Tiger logo to distribution partner symbols, with a man in a suit holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe. The voiceover confirms this symbolizes Onitsuka making 'deals with other distribution partners,' representing the brand's new alliances and the severing of ties with Blue Ribbon Sports.

Map of USA in Orange.description: A stylized map of the United States in orange, with the Onitsuka Tiger logo. The voiceover explains this emphasizes Onitsuka having 'gained a significant foothold in the United States,' highlighting the brand's market reach and the conflict over distribution.

Calendar with Appointments.description: A white calendar labeled 'APPOINTMENTS' with a purple calendar icon, showing dates. The voiceover specifies this represents 'a schedule of appointments to meet with them,' detailing the planning involved in the business operations.

Silhouettes of Executives.description: Purple silhouettes of men in suits, standing in a row, with text overlays indicating Onitsuka's decision to expand and move on from Blue Ribbon Sports. The voiceover clarifies these are 'the Onitsuka executives' who 'decided that they needed to move on to more experienced distributors to expand and increase their revenue,' explaining their strategic shift.

Animated Documents.description: A stack of beige documents labeled 'SHOE DISTRIBUTOR' with a purple sneaker icon, fanned out against a purple background. The voiceover specifies these contained 'a list of 18 athletic shoe distributors across the US,' symbolizing the distribution contracts and logistical efforts of Blue Ribbon Sports.

Fiery Explosion.description: A large, orange explosion with smoke, appearing at the end of the segment, with the text 'THEN IT WAS TIME FOR WAR.' The voiceover declares 'then it was time for war,' accompanied by an explosion sound effect, symbolizing the outbreak of direct conflict between Onitsuka and Blue Ribbon Sports.

Chalkboard with Diagrams.description: A dark chalkboard filled with white diagrams, notes, and labels like 'ARMORED TIGER' and 'BABY,' with a man in a suit standing in front. The voiceover confirms this symbolizes Onitsuka 'secretly plotting to replace Blue Ribbon,' revealing their hidden strategic agenda.

Woman at Counter.description: A woman in a brown coat handing a receipt labeled 'World's Toughest' to a man in an apron, symbolizing a transaction or challenge, possibly related to sales or product testing.

Man in Suit Declaring 'NIKE'.description: Identified as Jeff Johnson, their first employee, who proposes the name 'Nike' in a direct, clear male voice, explaining its origin as 'the Greek winged goddess of victory.'

Man in Suit Reviewing Documents.description: A man in a dark suit and tie, seen as the team considers new brand names, with 'DIMENSION' appearing on documents, referencing Phil's initial, unpopular suggestion of 'Dimension 6.'

Boardroom Meeting.description: A group of business professionals in suits, including co-founders Phil and Bowerman, gathered to strategize and begin working on creating their own original shoes, moving away from selling Onitsuka's Tiger shoes.

Nike Sneaker.description: A white sneaker with a red swoosh, shown in close-up with the text 'NEW VERSION,' specifically identified by the narrator as a 'new version of the popular Cortez,' symbolizing innovation and the evolution of the product line.

Diner Interior.description: A dimly lit diner with a sign reading 'Midway Sports & Loan,' featuring wooden booths and an American flag, serving as a setting for business discussions and financial planning related to finding a suitable shoe manufacturer.

Hourglass.description: A fiery hourglass with glowing orange flames, appearing in multiple scenes, symbolizing the urgent passage of time and critical decision-making moments in the brand’s origin story, particularly the immediate need to choose a name.

Woman in Brown Jacket.description: A young woman with long dark hair, wearing a brown jacket and a patterned scarf, walking through a sunlit hallway, representing the graphic design student who created the iconic Nike swoosh for which she was paid $35.

Man in Diner.description: The Man in Suit seated in a diner with 'Midway Sports & Loan' visible in the background, speaking earnestly, symbolizing the early business negotiations and financial challenges.

Shoe Workshop Worker.description: A man in a workshop operating a sewing machine, stitching a shoe, representing the hands-on production process and the importance of craftsmanship, whose work is later associated with 'QUALITY CONTROL ISSUES' faced by initial manufacturers.

Workshop Setting.description: A dimly lit workshop with industrial sewing machines and tools, where a man is seen stitching a shoe, emphasizing the manual labor and the recurring 'QUALITY CONTROL ISSUES' encountered during early shoe production.

raw_segments: The video opens with a collage of vintage black-and-white and sepia-toned images, including runners, a man in a suit holding a shoe, and a storefront labeled 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES,' overlaid with bold orange text stating '$300,000' and 'THAT BROUGHT SUCCESS TO BOTH PARTIES.' A male narrator immediately states, "300,000 dollars," explaining that "business was booming, and everything seemed to be going great between Phil's company, Blue Ribbon Sports, and Onitsuka in Japan." He continues, "Together, they'd created a partnership that brought success to both parties." The scene transitions to a purple-tinted image of a man in a suit and cap, juxtaposed with a sepia-toned man in a suit holding a shoe, symbolizing the partnership's success. The narrative then shifts dramatically to a green-tinted, digital-themed sequence featuring the Nike logo with question marks, followed by a silhouette of a man facing a glowing Earth and a cascade of money, symbolizing global ambition and financial growth. An ominous background music begins as the narrator reveals, "But what Phil didn't know was that Onitsuka was planning to stab them in the back." The music fades, and the narrator shifts to a reflective tone, stating, "Hearing this story of Nike shows how difficult it was for entrepreneurs to execute on their ideas back then. Phil literally started by selling shoes out of his car." The background music becomes upbeat and modern as the narrator introduces a solution: "But now, anyone with an idea for a product can start selling online quickly and easily with Shopify." The video cuts to a modern, brightly lit home office where a woman smiles at her laptop, transitioning to a screen displaying Shopify’s interface with the tagline 'If you can dream it, you can sell it with Shopify,' highlighting ease of online business creation. The narrator elaborates, "Shopify allows anyone, even those with no technical skills at all, to create, grow, and manage a business." He adds, "What I love about Shopify is how much time it saves you. In just a few clicks, you can create a stunning, professional-looking online store that's completely tailored to your business, and begin selling your products and receiving payments." The sequence continues with a man in glasses typing on a laptop, surrounded by floating dollar bills and green digital effects, reinforcing the theme of online entrepreneurship. The narrator shares a personal goal: "For me, having an online business had always been my goal. I wanted to be able to travel anywhere in the world and know that wherever I..." The final frames show a white silhouette of a man against a green background of money, with a shopping bag icon appearing over his head, symbolizing the culmination of an online business journey. The Shopify logo appears prominently, tying the narrative to the platform’s role in enabling success.

The minute opens with an upbeat, electronic background track as a male voiceover promotes Shopify, stating his business is making money thanks to the platform. Visually, a white silhouette of a man against a green, money-filled background with the Shopify logo transitions to a vibrant purple neon outline, followed by a close-up of hands counting money near a laptop. The scene then shows a green gradient screen with the Shopify logo and a call to action: 'CLICK THE LINK IN THE DESCRIPTION' with downward arrows, as the voiceover encourages viewers to start their own business with Shopify. A dramatic transition sound marks the shift to 'CHAPTER 5: BETRAYAL,' a title card set against sneaker sketches. The narrative continues with a male voiceover explaining that Phil, represented by the Man with White Hair on a split-screen with the Man in Suit holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe, asked Onitsuka if Blue Ribbon Sports could be the exclusive distributor for Tiger shoes across all of America. A map of North America with the U.S. highlighted in orange flashes 'NO!' as the narrator explains Onitsuka's initial rejection, believing Phil's company wasn't big or established enough, a point reinforced by towering skyscrapers under an orange sky with explanatory text. The narrator then recounts Phil's immediate, albeit false, claim, "But we do have offices on the East Coast," as the Man with White Hair reappears over a world map with the U.S. highlighted in yellow, followed by a bold red 'THEY DIDN'T' text overlay. Despite this, the Man in Suit is shown again with text stating 'ONITSUKA BELIEVED PHIL' and the Blue Ribbon Sports logo, as the narrator confirms Onitsuka was impressed and struck a deal for Blue Ribbon to be the exclusive US partner. The segment concludes with the Man with White Hair, a large '20,000' and Onitsuka Tiger shoes, symbolizing the successful sales milestone amidst the building tension of betrayal.

The segment opens with a purple-tinted map of the United States, overlaid with location pins and shipping boxes, symbolizing a distribution network for an order of "20,000 more pairs" of shoes. A narrator explains that an Onitsuka executive promised to ship these to Blue Ribbon's East Coast office, which, in reality, didn't exist. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and red tie, identified by the narrator as Phil, in a dimly lit office, as promotional flyers for 'Blue Ribbon Sports' appear with bold text: 'INVEST IN BRS NOW!' The narrator details how Phil sent these flyers to raise money for new offices, describing the company as a fast-growing shoe business looking to expand, where people could buy shares. However, the response was nonexistent—'NOBODY AT ALL RESPONDED'—leading to a moment of humiliation, visually represented by a man covering his face, while the narrator emphasizes the worrying lack of belief in their business. The narrative shifts to an older couple in a modest home, counting cash in bins, identified by the narrator as the parents of early employee Bob Woodell, who invested $8,000—their "entire life savings"—to help the company, despite a visual of '$95,000' highlighted in front of a bank facade. The narrator explains Phil's reluctance to take their money, but banks were refusing further loans, leaving him no other choice. The couple is then shown in an office meeting, while a man in a plaid shirt, Jeff Johnson, appears frustrated and later in a backyard, visibly distressed. The segment ends with a split-screen of two older men, one with white hair and a mustache, the other with a stern expression, with a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker superimposed between them, linking the financial hardship to the product and the initial supplier. A dramatic instrumental score underscores the entire narrative.

The segment opens with a dramatic, suspenseful background score as a male narrator explains the initial partnership. A split-screen shows two older men, identified as Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman, one with white hair and a mustache, the other with a stern expression. Overlaid with a white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker and text, the narrator states they 'loved the shoes created by Onitsuka' and 'respected the quality of the Japanese brand,' making it seem 'win-win for both sides.' The scene transitions to a patterned background of shoe silhouettes with the 'BRS' logo and 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS' text, followed by an orange map of the U.S. with 'Onitsuka Tiger' written across it, as the narrator details how Onitsuka became a 'formidable brand in the United States' thanks to Blue Ribbon Sports. A man in a suit, Phil, appears in a dimly lit, purple-tinted room, speaking with concern, followed by another man in a blue shirt gesturing thoughtfully, as the narrator reveals that despite Phil's belief in Onitsuka, 'they had some problems' that were 'about to get even bigger.' A Jenga tower labeled with 'A', 'B', 'BB', 'BBB' symbolizes this instability, with text explaining that Blue Ribbon Sports' initial success led to rapid expansion. A storefront with a 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES' sign appears, followed by the white-haired man (Phil) reappearing with text about cash flow risks, as the narrator explains Phil 'constantly had to worry about cash flow' to support new stores and employees. A man in a tan jacket walks through a dark, industrial setting with 'HIRE MORE STAFF' text, aligning with Phil's need to 'get more facilities, hire more staff, and order more shoes.' The Onitsuka Tiger sneaker reappears over a purple background with shoe silhouettes, then Phil, the man in a suit, gestures passionately as text states 'AND THEY WERE SELLING OUT FASTER THAN THEY COULD KEEP UP WITH,' with the narrator specifically mentioning the 'Cortez' model's popularity. A map of the U.S. and Japan appears with the sneaker flying between them, as the narrator explains Phil 'repeatedly tried to have Onitsuka send the shoes faster.' The final shot shows Phil holding a shoe with text 'ONITSUKA HAD BEEN SELLING TO THEIR JAPANESE CUSTOMERS FIRST AS THE PRIORITY,' followed by a cargo ship at sea with text 'AND SIMPLY IMPORTING WHATEVER WAS LEFT OVER TO THE US,' highlighting the supply chain conflict.

The segment opens with a dimly lit, purple-tinted close-up of the Man in Suit, identified by the narrator as Phil, his expression tense, as text overlays state 'THERE WASN’T REALLY MUCH PHIL COULD DO,' a sentiment echoed by the narrator who states, "It was unfair, but there wasn't really much Phil could do." The scene shifts to a dark warehouse filled with shoe boxes, with the word 'SUGGESTIONS' in bold orange, followed by a shot of Phil in a meeting, with text indicating 'THAT PHIL AND BOWERMAN SHARED WITH THEM.' A sepia-toned portrait of Bill Bowerman appears against a background of shoe sketches, with text noting his efforts to improve designs, as the narrator explains that Onitsuka was slow to produce the innovations Phil and Bowerman shared, sometimes completely ignoring their suggestions, which "infuriated Bowerman, who'd been working hard on new designs and improvements." The narrative continues with a formal dinner scene where two men in suits converse, overlaid with text about renewing the contract, as Phil, hoping to improve the situation, is then shown in a dark, blue-lit setting, with text revealing he arranged another meeting with Onitsuka. A silhouette of a man in a suit appears against a purple background, visually labeled 'THE MAN NAMED KITAGAWA,' but the narrator identifies him as "A man named Katami," describing the meeting as 'STRANGE' and noting that Katami said "Blue Ribbon sales were disappointing" and showed a lack of interest in renewing the deal. The scene transitions to a patterned shoe background with text about disappointing sales, then to a dimly lit office where Phil looks concerned, with text stating he felt his company had earned the contract, a feeling the narrator confirms, stating Phil "couldn't understand it" and "felt his company had definitely earned the contract extension." A map of the U.S. in orange appears with the Onitsuka Tiger logo, followed by a man in a suit standing before a fiery backdrop, with text about introducing the Tiger brand in America, as the narrator explains Onitsuka's success was "down to Blue Ribbon Sports" who introduced the "Tiger brand in America," with sales "growing every year." A close-up of a man in a suit shows text about 70% of American runners wearing Onitsuka shoes, with Phil having shown them a survey to prove it, followed by a shoe graphic and text about Bowerman’s design contributions, which the narrator confirms, adding that Bowerman "had helped improve their shoe designs." The scene shifts to a dark hallway with silhouettes walking, with text revealing Onitsuka’s plans to partner with other distributors, a suspicion Phil had already harbored, according to the narrator. The segment ends with a man in a tuxedo looking alarmed, with a red warning triangle and text 'SOMETHING WASN’T RIGHT,' emphasizing the growing sense of betrayal and conflict, as the narrator's voice fades, stating it was "becoming increasingly..."

The segment opens with a dimly lit office scene where a man in a suit, identified as Phil Knight, is seen in a tense meeting, reflecting the growing strain in the business relationship, as a voiceover states that 'something wasn't right.' After 'Katami left the room,' Phil took a folder from his briefcase. The narrative shifts to a series of animated graphics: a stack of 'SHOE DISTRIBUTOR' documents, which the voiceover specifies contained 'a list of 18 athletic shoe distributors across the US,' followed by a calendar labeled 'APPOINTMENTS,' symbolizing 'a schedule of appointments to meet with them.' A man in a suit, likely a key Onitsuka executive, is shown in front of a chalkboard filled with diagrams and notes, with text overlay stating 'IT WAS NOW CLEAR THAT ONITSUKA WAS SECRETLY PLOTTING TO REPLACE BLUE RIBBON,' a betrayal confirmed by the voiceover stating Onitsuka was 'secretly plotting to replace Blue Ribbon and find a new distributor.' A map of the USA in orange appears with the Onitsuka Tiger logo, emphasizing the brand's dominance and the conflict over distribution, as the voiceover explains Onitsuka had 'gained a significant foothold in the United States.' Text 'THANKS TO BLUE RIBBON'S EFFORT' appears, followed by silhouettes of executives with the message 'THE ONITSUKA EXECUTIVES DECIDED THAT THEY NEEDED TO MOVE ON TO EXPAND,' with the voiceover elaborating that 'the Onitsuka executives decided that they needed to move on to more experienced distributors to expand and increase their revenue.' A close-up of a man in a blue shirt, identified by the voiceover as 'Phil,' shows him in deep thought, reflecting the emotional weight of the situation, as the voiceover describes him as 'outraged' and 'hurt,' and unable to 'believe this was happening.' The narrative escalates with the Onitsuka Tiger logo and text stating 'EITHER WE BUY 51% OF BLUE RIBBON SPORTS OR WE MAKE DEALS WITH OTHER DISTRIBUTION PARTNERS,' presenting the ultimatum, which the voiceover explicitly states was given by 'the Japanese brand.' The Man in Suit (Phil Knight) is shown in a dimly lit setting, with text 'PHIL KNIGHT IMMEDIATELY REJECTED THE OFFER TO BUY HIS COMPANY,' highlighting his defiance, as the voiceover confirms 'Phil Knight immediately rejected their offer to buy his company.' The scene transitions to a handshake graphic between Onitsuka Tiger and distribution partners, with a man in a suit holding an Onitsuka Tiger shoe, symbolizing the brand's new alliances, which the voiceover describes as making 'deals with other distribution partners.' The final frame shows a fiery explosion with the text 'THEN IT WAS TIME FOR WAR,' marking the beginning of a direct confrontation between Onitsuka and Blue Ribbon Sports, accompanied by an explosion sound effect and the voiceover declaring, 'then it was time for war.' The lighting throughout is predominantly purple and dark, creating a dramatic and tense atmosphere, with text overlays in bold yellow and orange to emphasize key plot points.

The segment opens with an ominous, low-frequency hum accompanying a bold title card, 'CHAPTER 6: DON’T GET ANGRY, GET EVEN,' set against a dark background with faint sneaker sketches, establishing a tone of retaliation and strategic resolve. A male narrator explains that it took a while for 'Phil,' a Man in Suit seen gazing contemplatively at a cityscape from a dimly lit office, to get over the shock of a meeting, but he ultimately decided to 'strike back.' The narrator continues, detailing how 'Phil and Bowerman' realized that losing the Onitsuka Tiger brand, specifically the 'Cortez' (Onitsuka's best-selling shoe designed by Bowerman), wasn't the end of the world but an opportunity to be more than just a distributor. The scene shifts to a shadowy shoe warehouse and a close-up of the white Onitsuka Tiger sneaker, followed by a vintage 'BLUE RIBBON SPORTS TIGER SHOES' storefront, as the narrator questions why Blue Ribbon couldn't just make and sell their own shoes. A cargo ship sails across a purple-tinted sea, overlaid with text about importing 'whatever was left over,' visually underscoring the distribution issues that making their own shoes would resolve, as the narrator explains they wouldn't have to wait for shipments and could make as many as they wanted. The two co-founders, Phil and Bowerman, are then seen in a Boardroom Meeting, gathering their team to work on creating their 'own original shoes' instead of selling Onitsuka's. A woman hands a man a receipt, symbolizing a transaction, as the narrator mentions the need for a new name. A Man in Suit reviews documents with 'DIMENSION' in bold yellow text, referencing Phil's initial, unpopular suggestion of 'Dimension 6.' The narrator then introduces 'Jeff Johnson, their first employee,' who, in a distinct male voice, declares, 'Let's call it Nike,' as the text appears in bold yellow. Phil, the Man in Suit, is shown with an intense expression, reacting with a spoken 'What?' before Jeff Johnson begins to explain, 'It's the Greek winged goddess of victory,' as the sequence concludes, symbolizing the birth of the new brand.

The segment opens with a close-up of the Man in Suit, his expression serious, as an aerial view of a Mayan pyramid fades in behind him, symbolizing ancient victory and legacy. A narrator explains that the name 'Nike' came from the Greek wing goddess of victory, a connection that made a lot of sense for a sports brand. The scene transitions to a dimly lit room where the Man in Suit and a woman are seated, with bold white text overlaying the image: 'THE CONNECTION TO VICTORY CERTAINLY MADE A LOT OF SENSE FOR A SPORTS BRAND.' The narrative shifts to an airplane interior, where the Man in Suit sits pensively by the window, hand on his chin, while a younger man in a suit looks on, as the narrator reveals that the Man in Suit (Phil) wasn't initially keen on the name Nike, but they were running out of time and had to decide immediately to start making plans for their new shoe brand. A fiery hourglass appears, with the word 'NIKE?' in purple, emphasizing this time pressure. The scene cuts to a grand hall where the Man in Suit and another man in a suit stand beside a glowing hourglass, reinforcing the theme of time and decision, as the narrator concludes that, with few other options, Phil agreed to go with the name Nike. The Man in Suit is then shown in a well-lit room, speaking directly to the camera, his expression resolute, as the narrator explains the next step was needing a logo. The word 'NIKE' flashes in bold yellow and purple letters, followed by a scene of a young woman in a brown jacket and patterned scarf walking through a sunlit hallway, flanked by two men, representing the graphic design student who created the large white Nike swoosh, for which she was paid '$35'. The camera focuses on hands sorting through papers labeled 'T. Nishikawa' and 'Midway,' suggesting business negotiations, as the narrator details the need for a shoe manufacturer who could produce high-quality shoes at an affordable price to compete with Onitsuka. The Man in Suit is then seen in a diner, speaking earnestly, with 'Midway Sports & Loan' visible in the background, symbolizing these early business discussions and financial challenges. The scene shifts to a workshop where a man operates a sewing machine, stitching a shoe, with a close-up of a white Nike Sneaker with a red swoosh and the text 'NEW VERSION' appearing, which the narrator identifies as a new version of the popular Cortez. The final shot shows a man in a dimly lit workshop, working a machine, with the text 'QUALITY CONTROL ISSUES' in bold purple, as the narrator concludes that all the initial manufacturers they tested seemed to have quality control problems.

event_blocks.summary: In a dimly lit boardroom, the Man in Suit, Phil, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, gravely informs his employees that their partnership with Onitsuka has ended, leaving them independent and working on a new line called Nike, despite significant quality issues and a tight deadline with no room for error. Close-ups reveal concerned executives, including a man with gray hair and another with dark hair and a red tie, initially appearing defeated, fearing the end of Blue Ribbon Sports. Phil then shifts his tone, declaring it the moment they've been waiting for, as animated icons for logistics, product, and innovation appear. He elaborates on Onitsuka's past failures—late deliveries, mixed orders, and resistance to design input—framing the situation as liberation rather than a crisis. A palpable relief spreads through the now attentive employees, including a man with glasses and a red tie, as the mood transforms. The scene shifts to a dimly lit workshop focused on production and quality control, where a Shoe Workshop Worker in a striped shirt and apron operates a sewing machine under the watchful eye of the Man in Red Jacket, illustrating the collaborative effort to establish the new Nike brand.

Man in Red Jacket.description: A man in a red jacket, standing behind the Shoe Workshop Worker, overseeing the production process with a serious expression, suggesting a supervisory role.

Man in Suit.description: Phil, a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, who delivers critical and inspiring speeches, first announcing the end of the Onitsuka partnership and quality issues, then rallying his team by declaring this a moment of liberation for the new Nike brand.

Shoe Workshop Worker.description: A man in a striped shirt and apron, operating a sewing machine in a dimly lit workshop, focused on stitching a shoe, with another man in a red jacket observing closely.

Boardroom Meeting.description: A group of business professionals in suits, initially slumped and defeated, but later showing relief and renewed determination after Phil's inspiring speech, which also reveals the company's original name, Blue Ribbon Sports, and the new brand, Nike.

raw_segments: The segment opens in a dimly lit boardroom where Phil, the Man in Suit, wearing a dark suit and patterned tie, gravely addresses his employees. He announces, "Our partnership with Onitsuka is over, and we're completely on our own now," a statement reinforced by on-screen text. Phil reveals they are working on a new line called Nike but admits, "there are big problems with the quality," and with limited time, "there's no margin for error." The camera cuts between close-ups of Phil and concerned reactions from seated executives, including a man with gray hair and another with dark hair and a red tie, who initially appear slumped and defeated, fearing the end of Blue Ribbon Sports. Realizing he needs to inspire his team, Phil pivots his speech, declaring, "This is the moment we've been waiting for," as animated icons symbolizing logistics, product, and innovation flash across the screen. He passionately explains how Onitsuka's late deliveries, mixed-up orders, and refusal to implement design ideas held them back, urging his team, "Let's not look at this as a crisis. Let's look at this as our liberation." A wave of relief washes over the now attentive employees, including a man with glasses and a red tie, as the mood shifts. The scene then transitions to a dimly lit workshop, emphasizing hands-on production and quality control, where a Shoe Workshop Worker in a striped shirt and apron operates a sewing machine, overseen by a Man in a Red Jacket, symbolizing the collective effort to make the new Nike brand successful.

event_blocks.summary: Two hands shake over a document on a wooden table under warm sunlight, signifying a partnership as a narrator details establishing full production control with manufacturers. The scene shifts to a dimly lit workshop where a man in a yellow jacket works on a shoe amidst tools and machinery, with purple location pins indicating global manufacturing. A courtroom drama unfolds as a man in a suit faces Onitsuka's lawsuit for breaching a contract to sell only Tiger shoes, a fact highlighted on a legal document, while Phil Knight countersues, alleging Onitsuka attempted to break an exclusivity deal, leading to a settlement. In 1971, Blue Ribbon Sports officially became Nike, achieving one million dollars in annual sales, visualized by money raining down with 'NIKE' and '$1,000,000' overlaid. Nike's global expansion is depicted by a world map adorned with yellow swooshes, followed by the brand's evolution marked by a black screen with the green Nike logo.
